examples: The processes of arousal and concentration give attention its direction.
Arousal is at first general, with a flooding of impulses in the brain stem; then gradually the activation is channeled.
An early strain of research claimed that when we watch television, our brains mostly exhibit slow alpha waves—indicating a low level of arousal, similar to when we are daydreaming.
It was found to elevate arousal 63% of the time, compared with 13% for a placebo.
Less extreme cases of arousal addiction may go unnoticed or be diagnosed as an attention or mood disorder.
